data.title: Centene: Even Slashing Guidance May Not Be Enough
data.description:
Centene: 40% Drop! Well Deserved

You didn't manage to sell the stock of Centene Corporation (NYSE:CNC) before it plummeted 40% this week? So, what now? Should you rush in to catch the falling knife? Should you wait for the dust to settle first? Or should you accept your decision to hang on to it through the hammering?

To be clear, I think the market has already inflicted substantive damage on CNC as I pen this write-up. Why?

  • CNC has fallen by another 1.4% as we head to the Fourth of July weekend, while the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) reached another new high. Going the opposite way (against the market) has never felt so distinct, painful, and surreal for CNC holders.
  • The wipeout this week has taken CNC back to the days of early 2017. A reminder that the market has taken Centene's decision to withdraw its 2025 guidance extremely seriously.
  • Valuation for CNC has already collapsed to 5x on a forward earnings basis, significantly below the 7.3x that marks the -2 standard deviation zone under its 10Y average. Is that enough? I will address that subsequently.

Nevertheless, I hope to have impressed upon you that the market isn't foolish. The market is trying to price in even more uncertainties as execution risks on Centene's primarily Medicaid-driven business model are undergoing incredible challenges. Health insurance companies have endured a terrible 2025, with UnitedHealth (UNH) already dropping to multi-year lows.

And the fire has now spread to the leading Medicaid players, compounded by the budget cuts informed by Congress passing Trump's "One, Big Beautiful Bill," sending it to the Oval Office for him to sign into law. The moment of reckoning seems to have crystallized for health insurance companies under fire this year, taking hits after hits, and why should we expect Centene to be able to avoid them?

Centene: Brace For 50% Cut To 2025 Adjusted EPS

Next, Wall Street isn't foolish. Calls have been made to suggest that Centene's adjusted EPS could be slashed by 50% from its initial prognosis of $7.25 for FY2025 (which is, of course, no longer viable given the guidance withdrawal). Management has alluded to a reduction of $2.75 from its adjusted EPS, pointing to a downward revision to less than $5. Hence, the base EPS reset obviously afflicted the company's projections because it sets Centene back by nearly 40% from its initial outlook. And the stock fell by 40%? It was quite appropriate, right?

As a reminder, we should take into account that Centene cautioned that the downward revisions may not be over. First, I must accord due credit to management for helping to quantify the assessed impact on its adjusted earnings. Because that really helps the market to navigate with a little more clarity, as compared to asking investors to "guesstimate" how wide or deep the extent of the cuts could amount to. Now, we can focus on:

  • Additional impacts from the other seven marketplace states that Centene is involved with, but has yet to reflect the updated data into its calculations. However, it has already informed that downgrades should be expected, given the data on the current 22 marketplace states that spurred its decision to pull guidance.
  • Investors can now think about the anticipated pricing changes from next year. Analysts will also try and parse plausible clarity from management at CNC's upcoming Q2 earnings call on the guideposts for their modeling, and also afford insights into whether it could set the low watermark for 2025. In other words, I think investors may start asking questions about whether a recovery to the $5 mark for its adjusted EPS could be viable.

Still, several risks could still unhinge Centene's thesis, even as it has attempted to diversify beyond the Medicaid business. Notably, Medicaid comprises more than 65% of its revenue in Q1 over the past 12 months. Next, Medicaid's gross profit was about 33% of its total base in Q1 over the past 12 months. But, with the latest updates on slower growth and encumbered with higher cost headwinds on its marketplace businesses, the double whammy may impinge on any near-term recovery prospects for a while.

Therefore, for investors to think about whether investors could reasonably project $5 as the adjusted EPS hurdle for management to clear becomes increasingly pivotal. Analyst estimates have not reflected the full damage yet, so we need to be careful here and try to ascertain whether an inflection point from 2026 can be attained.

Buy Or Sell CNC Stock?

Note that 2026 adjusted EPS forecasts still point to $7.11 (according to S&P Cap IQ data). On a next 12 months basis, the adjusted EPS is still at the $6.6 level. I suspect these are no longer viable. Hence, if we assess $5 as a possible 2026 adjusted EPS for us to model, then CNC's 2026 P/E multiple should be reflected as 6.7x.

And if we compared it to the 7.3x level that marked its highly pessimistic zone, that's still a relative discount of about 8%. Does it sound reasonable enough? Well, I would say the market has arguably done its job, and that hammering is conceivably justified to reflect the downgrade. However, it's also clear that CNC has dropped to levels lower than 6x before, although not too often over the past ten years.

So, I think we must be circumspect when we contemplate that the regulatory and political environment for Centene's primary ACA and Medicaid business is facing noticeable structural constraints that could hinder a near-term recovery.

UNH stock has also struggled for traction amidst its own challenges earlier this year. All this while, the S&P 500 has been gaining incredible momentum to take out new highs, while health insurance investors suffer one fallout after another.

For now, I think it might be better to listen and attune to the market. While CNC is arguably cheap, it sure looks like it's cheap for (very good) reasons, with the hammering well validated and apt.

Rating: Hold.

data.title: Allstate: Progress Continues, But Too Soon To Buy
data.description:
Shares of the Allstate Corporation (NYSE:ALL) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 23%. The company has made significant progress restoring profitability to its key auto segment while improving operating efficiency. The company has also recently completed its second of two large divestitures that leave it well-capitalized and more focused. I last covered shares in February, rating the stock a “hold”, and since then, it has been a market performer, returning 3%. With these developments, now is a good time to revisit ALL.

Beginning with recent developments, on July 1st, Allstate completed the sale of its Group Health unit for $1.25 billion, which will result in a $500 million gain. This is a bit above its initial estimate of $450 million. Together with the previous sale of its voluntary benefits unit, Allstate has netted $3.25 billion of proceeds this year, which it can recycle into its higher-performing core business.

Post-COVID, the business was challenged by weak results in its auto segment as vehicle inflation soared, worsening the severity of claims. This strained the company’s capital position, forcing it to stop buybacks. With internal capital generation and these asset sales, Allstate has rebuilt its “stress” capital management buffer. As a result, it can resume buybacks with a ~$1.5 billion authorization in place. After $104 million of Q1 buybacks, I expect a $200-300 million pace going forward, which should roughly equal 1.5% of its share count. It also continues to pay a 2% dividend yield.

Given more difficult economics, Allstate had slowed activity in its auto unit, and as you can see below, auto policies ended Q1 about 0.4% lower than a year ago, whereas homeowners' policies continue to grow. Of its 38 million policies, 25 million are in auto, with about 8 million in homeowners, so auto is the primary driver of the business. While I do not see the business shifting to meaningful growth, I do see signs that we may be nearing a bottom.

As disclosed at a June conference, Allstate now believes it can earn targeted returns in every state where it operates, other than New York and New Jersey, where it continues to work with regulators to increase the rate level. In those two states, it is only maintaining existing customers and not adding new ones. However, everywhere else, Allstate is open to new policyholders, creating ample opportunity for modest growth. Given recent results, I share management’s belief that the company has sufficiently regained pricing to justify growth.

In the company’s first quarter, reported on April 30th, Allstate earned $3.53, which beat estimates by $1.00 as revenue grew by 8% to $16.5 billion. Now, Q1 results were of course negatively impacted by the California wildfire season. Allstate had $3.3 billion of California wildfire losses, against which there were $1.1 billion of reinsurance recoveries. As you can see below, the losses suffered in Q1 were unprecedented, as typically Q3 is the worst for cat losses given the US hurricane season.

Impressively, even with these losses, the company generated an underwriting profit. Allstate reported a 97.4% combined ratio, which was up 4.4% from last year. Catastrophe losses were a 10% headwind, with the underlying ratio improving by 380bps to 83.1%. Allstate has become increasingly efficient in its spending, which is one factor that has supported structural underwriting improvements. Thanks to its investments in digitization and consolidating its real estate footprint, it has reduced expenses by 6.7% since 2018

Auto premiums grew by 5% to $9.8 billion, while homeowners grew by 20% to $3.5 billion. This serves as another reminder that ALL’s business is much more auto-centric, with about 2.5x the premiums. The California wildfires were more of a challenge for housing-centric insurers, which is a reason why Allstate managed to turn a profit. Indeed, Auto earnings rose by $465 million to $816 million, with an excellent 91.3% combined ratio. In contrast, Homeowners lost $451 million as their combined ratio jumped 30% to 112.3% due to cat losses.

Low-90% is an excellent level for the auto business, and the company has finally restored sufficient pricing to deliver a reasonable underwriting profit. I think this story of margin improvement is now largely played out. Indeed, in June, management noted that it sees auto and homeowners as “increasingly competitive” now that margins are back to more normal levels. This is consistent with Allstate’s own behavior; after being closed in several states, it is now actively pursuing new customers, nearly everywhere.

As you can see below, insurance premium inflation is now moderating. It does remain above overall inflation levels, which is a positive for underwriting margins, but the majority of the margin recapture has likely occurred. Thus far, we have not seen a meaningful uptick in new and used vehicle prices from the new 25% auto tariffs. However, if we were to see car prices rise by more than 5%, that could cause another round of pressure on Allstate’s margins, though, as we have seen recently, I would expect it to gradually recoup this via higher premium pricing. This risk appears manageable.

As with most insurers, Allstate also has a large investment portfolio, and the company has been a prime beneficiary of higher interest rates. In Q1, Allstate generated $854 million in net investment income, up $90 million from last year, as it has continued to reinvest maturities at higher yields. It has also taken the opportunity to gradually extend the duration of the portfolio, which is now at about 5 years, insulating it from potential declines in rates.

Overall, Allstate has $60 billion of fixed income and $14 billion of return-seeking assets. Its fixed income portfolio is conservative and a source of liquidity for claims; 94% of the fixed income portfolio is investment grade. Over the past year, its alternative portfolio earned 4.5%, a weak outturn. Over the past five years, it has returned 12%, a strong level, as I like to see 8-12% returns to justify the incremental capital impact of riskier investments. With the market recovering, I expect to see an improvement in alternative returns. I would note that alt income is often reported on a one-quarter lag, so we are more likely to see improvement in Q3 than Q2.

Back in February, I saw the company having normalized earnings of $19.50, but closer to $17-$18 of reported 2025 EPS given the likelihood of excess catastrophe losses considering the California wildfires. In May, the company reported $614 million of after-tax catastrophe losses, bringing the quarter-to-date total to $1.08 billion. This is a more normal level of losses, but the big question remains what the US hurricane season delivers. It has been a quiet start, but devastating storms can occur through October, so it is too soon to know.

My base case continues to be the assumption of an average hurricane season, resulting in worse-than-normal cat losses. With buybacks a bit faster than I initially expected and encouraging auto profitability, I see earnings coming in at $17.50-$18.25 this year with a run-rate of $19.50-$19.75. That leaves shares just above 10x earnings, and its multiple continues to be weighed down by its relatively low capital return yield of ~3.5%.

A company like Chubb (CB) trades at 11x 2026 earnings. With its more diversified business and history of strong underwriting, I believe it does merit a higher multiple than Allstate, especially given how commoditized auto insurance is. Until we see the company more aggressively return capital to shareholders, which is unlikely before 2026, valuation is likely to be capped. Results continue to move in the right direction, I expect shares to be rangebound in the $195-$205 range as valuation is appropriate. As such, I continue to view ALL as a suitable “hold,” but would not rush to add unless we saw a pullback into the $180s.

data.title: Platinum ETFs: Beyond Gold And Silver
data.description:
While equities have rebounded from this year’s earlier pullback, investors are increasingly revisiting alternative investments like precious metals for diversification and downside protection. While gold and silver often dominate the precious metals narrative, platinum ETFs are quietly gaining attention. So far in 2025, platinum prices have increased almost 50% YTD, reaching their highest level in over 10 years. For investors and advisors curious about investing in platinum, this note provides an overview of growth drivers, market characteristics, and ETF investment options.

Platinum is relatively under the radar as an investment compared to gold and silver

While gold is primarily an investment asset and silver depends heavily on industrial use, platinum is a hybrid of both. According to the World Platinum Investment Council, 56-78% of platinum demand comes from automotive and industrial applications. Its industrial and automotive demand is followed by 23-29% jewelry demand. That demand driver has been strengthening due to the high sustained price of gold. And lastly, investment demand makes up the remaining (small) amount. This means platinum tends to perform well during periods of industrial recovery and technological shifts, while still offering some of the safe haven appeal behind precious metals.

While demand continues to remain healthy over recent years, supply has grown constrained. The WPIC predicts that platinum will hit its third consecutive year of supply deficit (see this article). Part of that is due to a large amount of production (70%) focused in South Africa that has recently declined. This is in contrast to gold production, where risk is more dispersed. According to gold.org, China is the largest producer of gold, with only 10% of global production.

Platinum as part of a diversified commodities allocation

Although gold will typically dominate precious metals exposure in portfolios, platinum’s demand drivers position it well for investors looking to diversify exposure across commodities. Platinum, however, also carries risks. The market remains relatively small and illiquid compared to gold and silver. And pricing can be highly sensitive to changes in automotive technology (platinum is typically used for traditional automobiles rather than electric vehicles) and mining supply disruptions. Still, for those with a longer-term horizon and an interest in industrial innovation and precious metals, platinum ETFs offer an interesting opportunity. Used in combination with gold and silver, platinum could potentially enhance a diversified commodities allocation.

While platinum has outperformed this year, it has modest inflows

Platinum ETFs have seen renewed interest this year. There are currently two physical platinum ETFs, which are significantly smaller than the largest gold and silver ETFs.

  • The largest physical platinum ETF, the abrdn Standard Physical Platinum Shares ETF (PPLT), has $1.6 billion in assets. It has received just over $100 million in net inflows so far this year. The fund is backed by physical platinum stored in secure vaults. It offers investors direct exposure to spot platinum prices without the complexities of futures contracts.
  • While the GraniteShares Platinum Trust (PLTM) is smaller, at around $81 million in assets. It has a lower expense ratio (50 basis points versus PPLT’s 60 basis points). Like PPLT, PLTM is backed by physical platinum. Both PPLT and PLTM store their platinum in London. Each fund discloses holdings regularly on their websites (PPLT currently has 11,410 bars, while PTLM currently has 860 bars).
  • Platinum can also be found in broader physical precious metals funds like the abrdn Physical Precious Metals Basket Shares ETF (GLTR). This fund holds multiple precious metals in physical storage. These include: gold (66.4% of net assets as of March 31, 2025), silver (26.6%), palladium (4.2%), and platinum (2.8%). Platinum is a small portion of this diversified ETF. So investors who want a higher allocation to platinum might prefer PPLT or PLTM. But GLTR can serve as a good hands-off approach to a diversified precious metals fund, especially for those who prefer a larger exposure to gold and silver.

Bottom Line

Platinum ETFs have performed well this year due to supply imbalances, and may also provide exposure to industrial growth potential in a diversified precious metals or commodity allocation.

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2024. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit VettaFi.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nestle: Business As Usual, And That's Not Good
data.description:
Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY) (OTCPK:NSRGF) shares continue to tread water as the company faces the reality of market saturation and a lack of growth opportunities.

I argued time and time again that the only path for market-beating returns for Nestlé is prioritizing margins over revenue, but the new CEO seems to be going in a different direction.

Nestlé is a “safe” investment in the sense that it doesn’t have significant downside risks, but I argue that upside is equally important, and Nestlé doesn’t provide it.

Slowly and quietly, Nestlé is a drag on stock-picker portfolios.

Let’s dive in.

Introduction To Nestle & The Burst Of The ‘Staple Bubble’

Nestlé shares are roughly flat over the past five years, significantly underperforming the S&P 500.

Nestlé is the perfect case of the ‘Staple Bubble’ I depicted in several articles. In short, I argue that the pandemic accelerated the saturation of the staple industry, which has been struggling to generate growth in the years that followed.

However, these staple companies represent a significant portion of broad indices, and are still perceived as safe havens, especially by older investors who are prioritizing risk mitigation and steady dividends.

As a result, many companies in the sector, Nestlé included, have traded at mid-twenties valuation multiples despite having little to no growth to support it.

Over the past few years, investors have slowly woken up to the fact that these companies can’t keep up with the market, and valuations have dropped to more reasonable levels.

Still, when accounting for growth, PEG ratios in the sector remain elevated, even compared to the type of companies that are commonly being discussed as overpriced.

Putting valuation aside, the question was, and remains, where do these staple giants find growth?

I long argued that the reality is that they have no meaningful growth avenues. The world population isn’t growing fast enough, and the geographic expansion runway is essentially non-existent.

As such, I believe that Nestlé and its peers should prioritize profitability. They should divest underperforming business lines and trim headcount significantly.

Well, that’s not what Nestlé’s new CEO seems to believe.

New CEO’s Strategy

Here’s the harsh truth. In my mind, Nestlé’s new CEO detailed a plan in the Capital Markets Day that is very much like saying “Let’s do good things and stop doing bad things”, or in other words, it’s a plan that’s only good on paper.

Obviously, every company should seek to achieve efficiencies, increase investments in winning brands, build new growth engines, etc.

The thing is, at this stage, I think that Nestlé has two paths. On one path, they go aggressive. They cut R&D spend, and they decrease headcount. They divest all struggling SKUs, take the L in the form of some write-offs, and start fresh with a portfolio of strong brands.

On another path, they continue to do what they are doing, which is essentially staying the course.

Under the first path, Nestlé might be able to achieve its targets of MSD% growth, with improving margins, which could very well lead to market-beating performance.

Under the second path, I don’t see how Nestlé achieves its targets.

In the five-month stretch from the Capital Markets Day in November to early April, Nestlé’s Swiss-listed stock jumped close to 20%.

Then, a selloff that started with tariff uncertainty was exacerbated with Q1 results that showed no sign of change.

Nestlé shares are now roughly at the same place they were before Capital Markets Day, reflecting the market’s skepticism about its ability to deliver its targets.

Note: ADRs are up over that stretch due to the weakening US Dollar.

Recent Results Show No Sign Of Change

First quarter sales were CHF 22.6 billion, up 2.8% organically, with pricing contributing 2.1%, and RIG growing 0.7%.

RIG has now been positive for four consecutive quarters, but it seems to be a matter of easy comps more than anything.

Looking at the different product categories, Confectionery led the way with 8.9% growth, followed by Beverages at 5.2%, Water at 2.9%, and Pet Care at 2.8%. Milk Products, Health Science, and Prepared Dishes were underperformers.

On a segment breakdown, AOA is the fastest-growing zone, followed by Europe, then the Americas, while Nespresso is the fastest-growing segment.

The devil is in the margins, which aren’t reported on a quarterly basis for Nestlé, but the meaningful contribution of pricing to growth is a testament to weak margins, as it was cited to be in response to higher cost inflation.

Again, investors in Nestlé acknowledge that a 1% movement in margin is far more meaningful than a 2%-3% improvement in growth.

It’s simple math, not a matter of preference.

Valuation & Prospects

I don’t see anything in Nestlé’s foreseeable future that will help it “get out” of the low-single-digit growth, on flat margins.

As long as that is the case, Nestlé won’t see its multiple expand, and it won’t generate above-average earnings growth.

At $99.6 per ADR and CHF 79 per Swiss-listed stock, Nestlé trades at roughly 19 times forward earnings.

Some might argue that this is a low level historically, but we’ve already discussed the mispricing of Nestlé back when it was trading in the mid-twenties.

In my mind, 19 times earnings, which is slightly below market-average, is between fair and overvalued, considering Nestlé’s growth prospects.

Conclusion

Nestlé is yet to prove, or even signal, that it’s turning the page on its low-growth and stable margins' trajectory.

The new CEO is currently delivering similar results to his predecessor, and he is not a magician. Nestlé is still a company facing market saturation and a lack of growth potential.

At 19 times forward earnings, Nestlé continues to be dead money in my view, mandating a ‘Hold’.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ZMMK:CA Safeguards Your Cash While You Wait
data.description:
All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise.

BMO Low Volatility Canadian Equity ETF (ZLB:CA) invests in large-cap, low-beta, Canadian companies. BMO Aggregate Bond Index ETF (ZAG:CA) invests in investment-grade bonds, both government and corporate kind. BMO Money Market Fund ETF Series (TSX:ZMMK:CA) holds a portfolio comprised of Canadian money market instruments. We wrote about the first two recently (here and here). We will talk about ZMMK:CA today.

ZMMK:CA is the ETF Series of the BMO Money Market Mutual Fund. The Fund's investment objective, according to its website, is capital preservation and to provide a high level of liquidity to its investors. The ETF Series started in 2021 and has close to $5 billion in net assets (the fund, which includes more series, has close to $6 billion). ZMMK:CA investors pay annual expenses of 0.13%, most of which is for management fees.

The actual expenses are quite a bit higher, but a portion is absorbed by the Fund company, so the unitholders only bear the 0.13%.

The Fund invests in a variety of money market instruments. Commercial paper and bearer deposit notes formed the bulk of it at March 31.

While we do not have the current breakdown, the above provides an idea of the types of investments held by the fund. The leading duo, at March 31, made up the majority of the portfolio even at September 30, 2024. In terms of industry allocation, most of the portfolio is comprised of issuances from the financial sector, followed by the industrial, energy, and utility sectors.

Money market instruments, by definition, mature in a year or less. Practically speaking, most money market funds aim for far less duration than even that. The portfolio manager of this fund targets a holdings composition with "an average term of 90 days or less to reset date and 180 days to maturity date". As we can see below, almost all of the investments mature in 180 days, with close to 40% turning over in under a month.

The portfolio manager only chooses securities that are rated R-1 (DBRS) or equivalent by the other rating agencies. Morningstar provides a good explanation for the R-1 rating from DBRS.

R-1 (-HIGH-) Highest credit quality. The capacity for the payment of short-term financial obligations as they fall due is exceptionally high. Unlikely to be adversely affected by future events.

R-1 (-middle-) Superior credit quality. The capacity for the payment of short-term financial obligations as they fall due is very high. Differs from R-1 (-HIGH-) by a relatively modest degree. Unlikely to be significantly vulnerable to future events.

R-1 (-LOW-) Good credit quality. The capacity for the payment of short-term financial obligations as they fall due is substantial. Overall strength is not as favorable as the higher rating categories. May be vulnerable to future events, but qualifying negative factors are considered manageable.

Source: Morningstar

The DBRS scale of the "Rs" is broadly convertible into the S&P system, as shown next.

As we can see below, the credit quality of the current portfolio meets the above criteria, protecting its unitholders from excessive credit risk.

ZMMK:CA pays 12 cents in distributions on a monthly basis. Annualizing that and applying it to the current price of the ETF ($49.87), gets us to a yield of 2.89%. At the beginning of 2024, it yielded almost twice that.

This did not come as a surprise to anyone, with the drop in the 3-month treasury bill yield being a precursor to the decrease in the ZMMK:CA payout.

ZMMK:CA, like most ETFs, distributes what it earns. We can see that from the annualized distribution yield and the weighted average yield to maturity numbers below.

True, this Series has expenses of 0.13%, so theoretically it should have around 2.75% of net income available to distribute. However, when we are looking at current numbers, timing differences can play a role in the short term. If you are looking for comparatives, then we can throw the iShares Premium Money Market ETF (CMR:CA) at you. This one yields about 3.09% today. The slightly higher likelihood comes from adding some unrated securities to the mix, as their current expense ratios are identical.

What Would You Use This For

Cash has been a forgotten instrument for some time. Bull markets tend to do that. But for those looking for a short-term parking space, this fund meets the criteria and checks all the boxes. You ideally want this if you are looking for 3 months' parking or less. If you know you need it parked for longer, then there are generally better choices available. We personally don't use funds like these, as most cash parking is during the waiting time for opportunities to invest. We tend to sell deep in-the-money covered calls at the strike we want to own stocks as a way of "cash parking". Generally, these have far better "yields," and we routinely earn at least 6% this way, even with outstanding margins of safety. But for those unfamiliar with how to do this, or for those who simply don't have time to micromanage their positions, ZMMK.CA does the best possible job in Canada.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Take The Labor Market Data With A Pinch Of Salt
data.description:
The labor market print for June surprised. To the upside. Defying consensus expectations. The stock markets ended the day with gains. A static monetary stance by the Fed can be the outcome later in the month from this, but caution is still warranted for stock market investors. A macroeconomic slowdown could be looming, calling for a focus on defensives. All of this is discussed below.

What the May Labor Market Report Says

  • Unemployment rate

After three months of staying unchanged, the unemployment rate actually dropped to 4.1% in June, compared to forecasts of a 4.3% rate. This is lower than the past 12 months' average of 4.16% and even the year-to-date (YTD) average of 4.13%, which is curious considering the Fed's latest projections.

Along with the June FOMC statement, the Fed also released its updated economic outlook, which has raised the expectation for the unemployment rate to 4.5% for 2025 from 4.4% earlier. This is in direct contrast with the latest data, leaving the question of whether the US economy could throw a positive surprise through the remainder of the year.

  • Non-farm payrolls

The rise in non-farm payrolls was also in sync with the unemployment rate, reflecting the ongoing resilience of the labor market. The number rose by 147,000 in June, an increase of 2% month-on-month (MoM) and almost in line with the average of 146,000. More notably, it was also 12.8% higher than the YTD average of the past 12 months and 25% higher than consensus estimates.

The revisions to April's and May's numbers also reflected sustained strength of the labor market. While the figure for April was revised up by 11,000, that for May is now up by 5,000.

Monetary Policy

In its monetary policy statements, the Fed routinely focuses on two economic indicators – the labor market and inflation. Considering this, the latest labor market data's strength doesn't make a case for any rate cuts. This further underlines what the reserve has pointed out about the recent weakness in GDP. That's due to the sharp impact of change in net exports, stemming from the proposed trade tariffs.

The latest data also indicates that the labor markets aren't getting any bit close to the Fed's projected unemployment rate for 2025. We will know more about how the economy responds only when the tariff implementation finally occurs, though.

For now, however, this leaves us with only the inflation numbers to consider, for which too, the Fed has raised its forecast (see table below). And indeed, the latest numbers for PCE inflation reflect some uptick. While headline PCE inflation came in at 2.3% YoY in May (April:2.2% YoY), similar to the uptick in headline CPI inflation for the month. Core PCE rose to 2.7% YoY (April:2.6% YoY).

So what we have in front of us now is sustained real economic health along with rising inflation, neither of which is conducive to a rate cut. In fact, with this as the background, it's surprising that there's any probability of a rate cut in the forthcoming FOMC meeting, as reflected in the chart below based on 30-day Fed Funds futures prices.

It does bear mentioning that the overwhelming probability is still tilted towards unchanged rates, which sounds about right. Conspicuous from its absence is also the small probability of a 25bps rate hike, which had shown up following the last CPI inflation release. This is likely due to the Fed's reduced GDP growth projections released a week later, which indicate the expectation of a deeper slowdown than earlier.

The Stock Market Response

The stock markets were clearly not displeased with the latest labor market data, with all three key indices slightly up at yesterday's close. The NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX, NASDAQ:QQQ) was up by almost a percent, while the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, NYSEARCA:SPY) and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) rose by 0.79% and 0.76%, respectively.

If at all the markets responded to the labor market data, which might not be the case given the decoupling seen in recent months, the reaction is in line with what to expect. Even if there's no chance of a rate cut, they do address a bigger worry right now, which is the state of the economy.

Where to Invest

But this buoyancy can't be taken for granted, which still makes a choice of stocks to buy more selective than in a bullish time. A sector-wise look at the stock markets, however, provides a good direction for the stocks to consider in my view.

At the time of the previous labor market release, three sectors were in the red YTD – healthcare, consumer discretionary, and energy. While the first two of these remain so, albeit to a smaller extent than at that time, energy no longer sees a pullback (see table below). Here, I focus on these sectors with some insights based on the current macroeconomic context:

  • Energy uncertainty

The uptick in energy was to be expected, going by the geopolitical stresses in the Middle East and their impact on oil prices as a result. But continued uncertainty around the oil play is something to consider even now. Before anything, profiting from rising prices during a crisis has a shrill ring to it, compared to rising prices from an economic boom leading to higher demand.

Next, whether or not oil prices continue to rise remains to be seen. Also, though labor markets and inflation point in the direction of strength, the weak forecasts for the US economy for 2025 are still concerning, especially as tariffs loom. If a slowdown does happen, it can affect oil prices. More details are available in my article on oil and gas producer Shell (SHEL) (check out the section "The oil price wild card").

  • Buy healthcare while it's still weak

Healthcare, though, especially with the weakness in the sector YTD, continues to look good. If the economy does take a turn for the worse, the defensive qualities of the sector can sustain portfolio health. But even otherwise, some of the stocks I've analysed recently look good.

For example, my most recent coverage on the Anglo-Swedish cancer treatments provider AstraZeneca (AZN) continues to reflect on the robustness of the company despite its ongoing challenges. Similarly, weight management treatment providers Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY) are well-placed too.

  • Patience is key with consumer discretionary stocks

Coming to consumer discretionary stocks, I have covered just one in the past month – Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) – which was rated a buy too. But patience would be a good idea right now with the stock in specific and the sector in general, considering that at least sentimental dips can be experienced if macroeconomic events lend uncertainty to the present environment.

In Conclusion

Labor markets surprised in June. Along with the latest inflation figures, they continue to indicate economic strength and unchanged policy rates at the forthcoming FOMC meeting. But this strength needs to be taken with a pinch of salt, considering the potential impact of tariffs on the US economy going forward.

Defensive stocks like healthcare ones are a good idea right now. This is even as cyclicals like energy see an uptick, but it's not for the right reasons, and there's no way of knowing if it will sustain. Even other cyclicals like consumer discretionary stocks need to be seen from a longer-term perspective for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I Am Feasting On These Bargain Dividends; Up To 9% Yields
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

You need a balanced diet to maintain a healthy lifestyle. This includes carbs for energy, protein for strength, healthy fats for function, and greens that are rich in micronutrients, antioxidants, and fiber — all of which support long-term health and lower disease risk. Your portfolio needs its own version of nutritional balance. You can’t call it a healthy meal if you consume carbs all day. Likewise, relying solely on Social Security for retirement is a poor and risky strategy.

The 30-year-old 4% rule recommends slowly selling a portion of your portfolio to fund your retirement. This method works, but is neither healthy nor sustainable, similar to a crash diet to quickly lose weight. When the economy faces recessions or prolonged periods of market price pressures due to a wide range of economic factors, the 4% rule will be lifestyle-compressing and financially stressful.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we believe in a diversified “dividend diet” —steady, recurring income from a mix of reliable sources that each perform differently across varying market conditions. Some holdings thrive during bull markets, while others shine in recessions. Certain positions benefit when energy prices surge, while others perform best when commodity prices fall. Some are built for high-interest-rate environments; others do better when rates are low. Our blend of income sources helps deliver consistent cash flow, nourishing your financial life through thick and thin.

We will now look at two picks that are out of favor for different reasons. The prevailing conditions have led to both experiencing downward price pressure. Nevertheless, they pay great income to incentivize us to stay invested, and offer terrific total returns when the cycles shift in their favor.

Pick #1: LYB – Yield 8.8%

LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (LYB) is a global leader in the chemical industry, developing and supplying materials that enable packaging, healthcare, agriculture, transportation, and industrial activities critical to our everyday living. The company is also the world’s largest licensor of polyolefin technologies, with over 350 licensed lines worldwide. This means that many other manufacturers pay LYB for the right to use its proprietary technology to produce polyolefins.

Being a downstream company, LYB operates in a cyclic industry and is experiencing margin pressures due to global overcapacity, weaker global demand, higher production costs (especially in the E.U.), volatile raw material costs, lower gasoline crack spreads, and other factors. The cyclical nature of the chemical industry means downturns are inevitable; the mark of a truly "high-quality" company like LYB is how it strategically manages and optimizes during these challenging periods to emerge stronger. The company recently shut down its 100-year-old refinery in Houston, with a pending decision around the use of this site for its MoreTech polymer recycling facility. LYB also ended its Dutch propylene oxide/styrene monomer production unit (Joint Venture with Covestro) at the Maasvlakte plant in the Netherlands. This approximately 22-year-old plant has experienced intermittent shutdowns and closures for several years due to economic reasons. Additionally, earlier this month, LYB entered into an agreement with AEQUITA to sell four of its select European O&P (olefins & polyolefins) assets (that were under strategic review) in Berre (France), Münchsmünster (Germany), Carrington UK, and Tarragona (Spain). The company projects this sale to increase its historical average EBITDA margin by ~3% and improve cash conversion. Further, this will materially increase LYB’s overall O&P exposure to cost-advantaged regions like the U.S. and the Middle East. Source

Speaking of cash conversion, a financial metric that measures how effectively a company converts its profits into actual cash flow from its core operations, LYB has maintained a fairly steady performance over the past five years, indicating strong execution and a resilient business model through economic cycles. Source

LYB maintains BBB investment-grade ratings from leading agencies and ended Q1 with $1.9 billion in cash and cash equivalents. The company spent $433 million on dividends and $110 million on share repurchases during Q1. Notably, the company’s shares outstanding have shrunk by 44% in the past 13 years, while dividend payments have grown over 240% (with two special dividends), indicating excellent value creation for shareholders.

Amidst the tariff uncertainties and off-cycle headwinds, LYB continued its track record of dividend growth, announcing a 2.2% YoY raise in May, marking its 15th consecutive year of dividend increases. Its current $1.37/share quarterly dividend reflects an 8.8% annualized yield, which is a Qualified Dividend Income (QDI), and does not incur foreign tax withholding due to LYB being a U.K. corporation for tax purposes.

Pick #2: SCHW Preferreds – Yield 6%

The Charles Schwab Corporation (SCHW) had its humble beginnings in 1975 as a discount broker at a time when brokerage commissions were deregulated. Today, SCHW is one of the largest brokerage firms in the United States, with $10.3 trillion in assets under management and over 37.4 million active brokerage accounts on its platform. In addition to brokerage accounts, the company also handles 5.6 million workplace plan participant accounts and 2.1 million banking accounts.

During May, the company reported 336,000 new accounts, representing a 7% increase from May 2024. SCHW’s customers are quite active on the platform, with the firm reporting its fifth consecutive month of daily trading volumes exceeding 7 million.

SCHW reported a regulatory Tier 1 Leverage Ratio of 9.9% (up from 8.8% at the end of Q1 2024), and the financial institution pursued $1.5 billion worth of common stock repurchases during the quarter while delivering an 8% YoY increase to the dividend. Its $595 million common dividend spend places the yield at a comfortable 31% payout ratio.

  • 5.95% Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock Series D (SCHW-D) – Yield 6%

  • 4.45% Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock Series J (SCHW-J) – Yield 5.8%

The company maintains a 35-year history of maintaining or growing its common dividend. During Q1, SCHW reported $1.9 billion in net income and spent $111 million on preferred stock dividends. The company reported $62 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash at the end of the first quarter. We note that SCHW preferreds carry investment-grade ‘BBB’ ratings from S&P. At current prices and elevated interest rates, we see a safer opportunity for strong total returns from SCHW-J preferreds, which offer a ~6% QDI yield and present ~30% upside to par.

Conclusion

Investing can be a lonely pursuit, especially with serious money at stake. The decisions are weighty, the risks real, and the stress can build up fast.

Why do it alone?

At High Dividend Opportunities, you don’t have to. We have a vibrant community of over 7,000 income investors who share ideas, support, and educate each other, and collectively help navigate the ups and downs of markets with confidence.

A balanced diet supports a long, healthy life, and a well-diversified income strategy can support a long, healthy retirement. Our model portfolio comprises a wide range of asset classes to draw income from across the spectrum of the financial markets. Invest the way you deserve to live: well-fed, balanced, and stress-free. This is the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Louisiana-Pacific: Fair Valuation For A High-Quality, Under-The-Radar Building Materials Giant
data.description:
Introduction

Louisiana-Pacific (LPX) is a rather boring manufacturing business at first glance, specializing in the production of building materials. Despite the fact that Warren Buffett owns around 8% of the company through Berkshire Hathaway, the company has attracted very little media attention in the past.

Since my last analysis on April 11, 2023 at a share price of $56.81, the company has outperformed the S&P 500 by ca. 16% with a total return of 68%. With this article, I would like to give an update on my original investment thesis.

To better understand Louisiana-Pacific, it can be broken down into three segments:

  • Siding – the hidden jewel of Louisiana-Pacific
  • OSB – a rather unattractive commodity business
  • South America – combines OSB and Siding in South American markets.

Since the segments differ significantly in their underlying economics, I believe it makes little sense to value the segments uniformly.

As a result, as I did back in 2023, I will evaluate the individual businesses with a sum of the parts analysis in the course of the analysis.

Siding

Let's start with the Siding business, which accounts for ca. 55% of total revenues. Within this segment, the company sells engineered wood products that are primarily used for lap, trim, fascia, soffit and sheds, but also in other smaller markets.

The share of revenue in this segment is 40% new construction, 40% reconstruction & repair (R&R) and 20% sheds and other ancillary markets.

Siding is a rapidly growing part of Louisiana-Pacific, which you can see in the numbers below:

Noteworthy is that:

  • Siding has achieved sales growth of + 7% p.a. and EBIT growth of + 11% p.a. since 2005, with an EBIT margin of 20% in FY 2024
  • Prices have not been lowered in any year, and have been raised on average by ca. 4% p.a.
  • The business operated with an ROIC of > 30%.

This is definitely a very good business! But why?

One of the main reasons is the structural advantages of engineered wood products compared to the substitutes vinyl, wood and fiber cement.

Over the past decade, engineered wood and fiber cement has gained significant market share from vinyl and wood siding. Wood has been losing market share for several decades because it simply cannot compete with vinyl, engineered wood and fiber cement (due to lower cost, easier installation and easier maintenance). Vinyl had its heyday in the mid-2000s and at that time had about 40% market share. Since then, engineered wood and fiber cement have become increasingly popular and have over time replaced vinyl. While vinyl has a right to exist as the lowest cost product, engineered wood and fiber cement deliver a better overall value proposition, primarily because of higher resistance (Vinyl is prone to cracking, especially in cold weather), better heat tolerance and longer warranty (LP SmartSide often comes with a 50-year limited warranty).

As a result, the market share of wood and vinyl structurally declined since the 2000s, while the market shares of fiber cement and engineered wood increased, as you can see below:

It is important to note that this graphic does not include engineered wood, which has grown even faster than fiber cement:

Within engineered wood and fiber cement, Louisiana-Pacific (engineered wood) and James Hardy (fiber cement) are clearly the dominant suppliers, with market shares of > 85% and > 90% in their respective markets in North America.

Both companies have been the subject of fierce competition for several years as to who can secure the largest share of the pie from wood and vinyl. The leadership of both companies even goes so far that some industry experts are drawing comparisons to Coke and Pepsi in the battle between LPX and JH. Either you're there for team Hardie, or for team Louisiana.

Between the two companies, I believe LPX's products offer structural advantages over JHX's as Louisiana's products are easier to handle and cut (thus cheaper and easier to install) and less prone to chips and breaks. Also, engineered wood siding is more environmentally friendly, but that's another discussion. While JHX are better in some areas due to superior fire resistance and insect resistance, this is only useful in some smaller markets.

Accordingly, Louisiana's volume growth has clearly outperformed that of James Hardy since 2015:

Given Louisiana-Pacific's product strengths, I expect that the company will continue to gain market share from vinyl, wood and fiber cement.

In my opinion, the possibility of further penetration still exists for many years to come. With a total market size still estimated at $15 billion, the siding segment of Louisiana captures only 10% of the total market based on total revenues of ca. $1.5 billion. The competing products hold a market share of:

  • 25% for vinyl, or ca. $3.75 billion
  • 5% for wood, or ca. $750 million
  • 22% for fiber cement, or ca. $3.3 billion.

This puts the incremental revenue potential for Louisiana-Pacific's siding products at almost $8 billion, or >5x today's revenues, assuming the market does not grow in the future.

The business also continues to benefit from:

  • A growing R&R market, supported by the aging of the North American housing stock, which means that the overall market will face structural tailwinds in the coming years
  • Penetration of new products such as ExpertFinish (ca. 13% of total sales) and BuilderSeries, which should raise the margin profile in the long term, as these products are both higher priced and have higher margins than existing products.

The current EBITDA margin of 25% should therefore increase over time.

How about competition? It's very unlikely that other OSB producers will come up with the idea of entering the market. According to statements made by the two largest competitors, West Fraser and Weyerhaeuser in personal conversations, the main reason is the lack of expertise, as siding involves a complicated technology. In addition, there are opportunity costs and project risks (including brand building). The same factors also apply to non-OSB competitors.

To conclude, the siding business is a great business with a high ROIC, high pricing power and a high moat that can still grow EBIT conservatively in the high single digits to low-teens range for many years to come resulting from the:

  • Aforementioned annual mid-single digit price increases
  • Expected mid single digit to high single digit volume increases
  • Shift to higher margin products.

But what is the value of the business?

Valuation Assumptions

I will value the siding segment based on a DCF model using a three-case scenario. Currently, the business generates ca. $240 million in Free Cashflow.

Regarding the growth value in the "best case" scenario, I use a 12% CAGR in the first two years and assume that growth will slow down over the years. I consider the growth rate to be very reasonable due to the factors mentioned above. In the "worst case" scenario, I use a CAGR of 8%. This is based on the assumption that I believe the business is capable of high single-digit growth even in a worst-case scenario due to its structural advantages and TAM opportunity.

Last but not least, I use a FCF multiple of 15x in the "best case" and 13x in the "worst case". Since usually companies with the corresponding growth rates will be valued with even higher multiples, I consider this to be reasonable. This leads to the following valuation:

Thus, the value of the Siding segment represents ca. 82% of the current market cap of ca. $6.4 billion.

OSB

OSB is the cyclical part of the business. OSB is the second-largest segment of Louisiana-Pacific, accounting for around 35% of sales, and comprises LPX's North American OSB business. The business is not very attractive by nature, as OSB is a very cyclical commodity product. Since OSB is primarily installed in new construction, selling prices are very sensitive to U.S. housing starts, which are known to vary widely across different stages of the economy.

Historically, the industry has been dominated by classic boom and bust cycles, where producers generate high profits in times of high demand and undersupply, and in some years record very high losses in times of low demand or oversupply.

Historical results of this segment are shown below:

In good times, such as the last five years, Louisiana-Pacific achieved EBITDA margins of up to 64%, while in bad times, such as the 2008 financial crisis, losses of up to $200 million had to be booked. The normalized EBITDA is around $300 million.

Important market developments in recent years have been that:

  • The market itself has developed into an oligopoly – the largest four players account for 70% of the market, compared to < 50% in 2005
  • Companies largely seem to act rational – they have learned from the past and have made almost no effort to increase the total market production capacity during the last peak
  • Louisiana-Pacific shifted its business to a higher share of Structural Solutions - a value-added OSB product that currently has a > 50% revenue share within the OSB segment, which is expected to increase to 75% long-term, vs. 25% during the financial crisis.

Overall, this has led to down cycles becoming less painful, as the chart below clearly shows:

On the upside, less capacity and less competition means quicker price hikes, higher profits and longer lasting peaks. According to Brad Southern, the CEO of LPX, the capacity of the industry today is only designed for 1.5-1.7 million new housing starts, while it was designed for 2.2 million housing starts at the time of the financial crisis. West Fraser even believes that supply shortages can be reached with as little as 1.4-1.5 million new housing starts. In contrast, the long-term historical average for new housing starts is around 1.4-1.5 million, while over the past decade, it has been significantly lower, partly due to the overheating pre-GFC. Currently, new housing starts in May were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of around 1.25 million, way below the historical average.

So, the bull case for this segment is that in the medium term we'll get back to a situation where houses reach the historical average (undersupply can't last forever), resulting in abnormal cash flows for the entire industry. If you look a few years into the future, despite near-term headwinds, it is very likely that this point will be reached again in the medium term, driven by:

  • Undersupply
  • Population growth.

All in all, the OSB industry is basically an oligopoly with high upside potential in good times, as well as less painful and shorter downside cycles in bad times. So how can the business be valued?

Valuation Assumptions

In my SOPT valuation of the OSB segment, I will use the peak FCF based on the historical results of the segment and apply an appropriate multiple to it. Looking at the annual reports from 2005-2024, the OSB segment stand-alone has been able to generate around $300-$700 million in FCF at peak times. I will use $500 million for the SOPT valuation, as this represents the average peak FCF. Additionally, I will use a peak FCF multiple of 3x in the "worst case" scenario and a multiple of 4x in the "best case" scenario. This should be appropriate due to the high cyclicality of the business. Based on the inputs above, I obtain the following fair value for the OSB segment:

This should be a very conservative valuation considering potential future tailwinds and the fact that the business is perfectly capable of generating over $800 million of FCF in very good years that are increasingly likely going forward.

South America

This segment is rather insignificant, with a total revenue share of less than 10%. Based on conservative assumptions, I estimate the value of the business to be around $240-$300 million with a FCF result of around $30 million in FY 2024 and using a FCF-multiple of 8-10x:

Louisiana-Pacific SOTP Valuation

If we put all the parts together, we get a SOTP valuation of $5.8-$8.7 billion for the whole business:

Thus, I consider Louisiana-Pacific to be fairly valued.

Risks

New housing starts continue to fall (perhaps even to <1 million levels) and remain at low levels for an extended period of time. This would invalidate my thesis, at least in the short term, but would not change the attractiveness of the business in the long term. The OSB segment has historically been able to operate profitably with as little as 800k restarts. So, even in this case, the company should be able to remain profitable after responding to changes in price and demand.

A competitor is launching a product that will disrupt LPX's siding products or OSB products. I think this point needs to be followed closely. I consider the risk in both segments to be possible, although not very likely in the medium term (especially in OSB as the product has been the standard for the last 50 years). However, this would definitely invalidate my thesis!

Other OSB producers are entering the siding market with competitive engineered wood products. This could pose a greater risk. Even if the barriers to entry are extremely high and it's very unlikely, this nonetheless needs to be monitored.

And finally, an overestimation of the siding segment. If the siding segment turns out to be less attractive than expected, this also invalidates my thesis.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Louisiana-Pacific is currently fairly priced by the market.

However, due to the high quality of the siding business, which offers a very good downside protection, as well as the high quality of the management, I remain invested and will be happy to take advantage of price drops as an opportunity to buy more shares of the company.

data.title: My Biggest New Investment For H2 2025: Blue Owl Capital
data.description:
Written by Jussi Askola for High Yield Investor.

In early 2025, I explained that my biggest investment for the year was Patria Investments (PAX). The alternative asset manager was experiencing rapid growth, yet it was offered at a very low valuation relative to its close peers.

Since then, its stock has already surged 27% and significantly outperformed its close peers:

I am bringing this up because I think that Blue Owl Capital Inc. (NYSE:OWL) is today offering a similar opportunity.

These are not empty words, as I have bought so much of it lately that it has become my biggest new investment for H2 2025.

What Is Blue Owl Capital?

Just like Patria, it is an alternative asset manager that earns fee income in exchange for managing investments on behalf of other investors.

The biggest company in this space is Blackstone (BX), and it has been very rewarding to its investors over the long run, as it was able to scale its assets under management, and the fee income that comes with it:

What makes such businesses so rewarding is that they are capital-light, meaning they can grow at a rapid pace without requiring much of their own capital, and they earn highly recurrent fee income from their management services.

If you can convince investors to let you manage their investments, this can then turn into a very lucrative business, and I think that Blue Owl is on the cusp of such rapid growth.

It has guided to more than double its assets under management ("AUM") by 2029, and this is expected to result in 20%+ annual growth in its fee-related income:

Moreover, we believe that this guidance is conservative, as this management has a history of underpromising and overdelivering. It has actually grown a lot faster than this since going public, and it focuses on high-growth investment verticals that are today attracting a lot of capital.

This includes direct lending, GP minority stakes, and data centers.

Blue Owl is a global leader in all of these asset classes. Here is how fast it has grown since its IPO:

And here is why I like its stock so much:

Assuming no multiple expansion, this targeted growth has the potential to 2.5x its share price in five years.

But add to that the near 5% dividend yield and some multiple expansion, and you could reach a 3–4x in just five years.

The company has a slide about this on their investor deck:

And we have good reasons to believe that some multiple expansion is warranted here, given that Blue Owl currently trades at a discount to its peers, despite enjoying many qualities that warrant a premium valuation:

1) Safer cash flow: Blue Owl manages nearly exclusively permanent capital, and it derives its revenue from management fees, rather than incentive fees. The management has previously said that "permanent capital means we keep the AUM we have already raised – unlike peers, we don’t have to raise $2 to grow AUM by $1." It is a huge advantage. It also results in 3x more fees on average for each $1 of capital raised.

2) Faster growth: In addition to that, Blue Owl's biggest strategies enjoy significant growth tailwinds. Most notably, private credit is exploding in popularity, and there is enormous demand for data center investments. Blue Owl holds high exposure to these rapidly growing sectors, while many of its peers, like Brookfield (BAM), are more heavily invested in traditional sectors like office buildings and malls.

3) Stronger balance sheet: It has less leverage and more liquidity than many of its peers, and this is especially true when you take into account the fact that its cash flow is more resilient and growing at a faster pace. Unlike some of its peers, which have funded significant co-investments with their own balance sheets, Blue Owl has maintained a capital-light model with low leverage. It has a BBB investment grade rating and no major debt maturities until 2031.

4) Superior management alignment and track record: The management owns 36% of the equity, and they are very friendly to shareholders, focusing on growth on a per-share basis, avoiding dilutive equity raises, and focusing on growing the dividend and achieving strong total returns. Their track record speaks louder than words, as they have massively outperformed their close peers on all fronts since going public:

So, Why Is It Cheaper Today?

Just like Patria, I think that it is because the company went public just a few years ago, and it is still overlooked by most investors.

The listing also served as a liquidity event to some of its legacy owners, and this has led to some selling pressure that may have prevented it from reaching a higher multiple so far.

Finally, many of its large peers are part of the S&P 500 (SPY) and this has surely helped them reach a higher multiple, whether it is justified or not.

The good news is that Blue Owl is now eligible to be included in the S&P 500, and a future inclusion could serve as a strong catalyst for the stock:

Risks

The biggest risk would be a major credit event similar to the Great Financial Crisis. A dysfunctional financial system could then lead to a surge in default rates on its lending business, impacting its earnings and its market sentiment.

Blue Owl has a great track record in this space and should do fine in a regular recession, but it could suffer a major setback if we faced a real financial crisis.

Private lending can be rewarding, but you don't get those higher interest rates without taking some risks.

Closing Note

Ultimately, even without any multiple expansion, Blue Owl is well-positioned to generate strong returns from its 20 %+ expected annual growth rate and its near 5% dividend yield.

Barring any major bad news, multiple contraction seems unlikely, but future multiple expansion seems like a strong possibility and would only add to the upside.

The resulting risk-to-reward is very attractive, and this is why I have built a large position in the company.

data.title: Rare (Financial) Independence Day Specials: Very Undervalued Blue-Chip 10%+ Yields
data.description:
To my fellow American readers: Happy Independence Day! While we celebrate the blessings we enjoy thanks to the hard-won independence that our founding fathers earned for us, I wanted to share with you two compelling opportunities to advance your financial independence through dividend passive income.

It is fairly rare to find blue-chip stocks trading at attractive valuations, especially during a time when the broader markets are feeling a lot of optimism, as they are right now. It is even rarer to find a blue-chip stock that is undervalued and offers a double-digit dividend yield that should be sustainable for the foreseeable future. Yet, right now Mr. Market is offering a number of these opportunities, and in today's article I am going to discuss two in particular that look attractively priced at the moment.

Rare Buying Opportunity #1

The first one I will talk to you about today is Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL). MSDL matches virtually all the criteria for being a blue-chip BDC (BIZD), in that it is managed by one of the leading banks and asset managers in the world, Morgan Stanley (MS), which has strong shareholder alignment given that it has an 11.1% equity stake in MSDL.

Additionally, MSDL has a track record of steadily growing its base dividend payout—up by 25% between when it first started in 2020 as a private BDC to today's payout. On top of that, it has one of the most attractive fee structures in the BDC space among externally managed publicly traded entities. It also has one of the most defensively postured investment portfolios, with 98% of the portfolio invested in senior secured loans and 96.3% of the total portfolio invested in first-lien senior secured loans, as well as 95% of the portfolio invested in non-cyclical industries.

The portfolio is very well diversified across 210 borrowers and 34 industries as well, and the loans are fairly conservatively structured, with just a 40% weighted average loan-to-value on them. This conservative underwriting, as well as MS’s quality as an external manager, is evidenced by the very low non-accrual rate in the portfolio of just 0.2% at cost, giving it one of the best-performing portfolios at the moment in the entire industry.

On top of its quality and defensively postured portfolio, MSDL has an investment-grade balance sheet with a conservative net debt-to-equity ratio of just 1.07 times, which gives it substantial additional leverage capacity to reach the mid-range of its target ratio between 1.0 times and 1.25 times of leverage. Additionally, it has very little debt maturing in the near future, and even the debt that is maturing does so in September of this year at a 7.55% interest rate. This means that refinancing it at a much lower rate will not be a problem, which means it will actually be a tailwind to the company's net investment income (NII) per share.

When you combine that tailwind with the potential additional NII per share tailwind of adding a little bit more leverage to reach their target range, it means that MSDL should be able to add considerable additional earnings power moving forward. This is important because in the most recent quarter, net investment income per share came in at $0.52, which only covered the base dividend with a 4% cushion. However, with the Fed appearing in no rush to cut rates aggressively and these additional NII per share tailwinds, MSDL's dividend payout should be quite sustainable for the foreseeable future.

When you combine all of that with the fact that it currently trades at a 7.3% discount to its net asset value—despite very similarly structured peer Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), which has a similar fee structure and portfolio construction as well as underwriting performance—commanding a very substantial 14% premium to net asset value, MSDL looks quite attractively priced right now. This makes it a 10.44% yield backed by a blue-chip investment portfolio, underwriting, and balance sheet quality that is a rare, materially undervalued buying opportunity.

Rare Buying Opportunity #2

Another very attractive 10%+ yield right now is the Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC). While Kayne Anderson is not quite as renowned as Morgan Stanley (MS) is, it still has had some success with the relatively popular Kayne Anderson Energy Infrastructure Fund (KYN), proving itself to be a reasonably skilled fund manager. However, what makes KBDC particularly attractive right now is that, like MSDL, it has a very defensively positioned portfolio with 98% of its investments in senior-secured first-lien loans.

It also has one of the most conservatively postured balance sheets in the BDC space, with a 0.86 times debt-to-equity ratio at quarter end. This means it has enormous additional capacity to get to its 1.0 to 1.25 times leverage target in the coming quarters, which means that it should enjoy a very powerful NII per share tailwind. This is important because its NII this past quarter was $0.40 per share, which is exactly in line with its regular dividend. However, the uplift from this significant additional leverage that it can add should enable it to easily cover its dividend payouts moving forward.

On top of that, its portfolio is pretty well diversified across 116 companies and 30 industries, and it will only get better diversified in the coming quarters as it adds additional investments by increasing its leverage ratio. Moreover, its counterparties are pretty high quality, given that they are 99% private equity-sponsored companies as there is a 4.2 times weighted average net leverage ratio on them, which is fairly reasonable, and 100% of them have financial covenants. There is also a 3.2 times weighted average interest coverage ratio on its loans.

Meanwhile, on a fair value basis, non-accruals are at 1.6%, which is higher than MSDL’s but still quite low compared to many peers in the industry. On top of that, the company’s balance sheet is in strong shape. It has no debt maturing until 2027, and that debt is currently at an 8.65% fixed interest rate. Its debt maturing in 2028 is at an 8.74% fixed interest rate. Given where interest rates are today, it is highly likely that they will be able to refinance that maturing debt in both cases at materially lower interest rates. This means that rather than facing a headwind from interest rates as it refinances its debt, it actually should be enjoying a tailwind.

This contrasts sharply with peers like BXSL, Ares Capital (ARCC), and others that are facing headwinds from having to refinance maturing fixed-rate debt at materially higher interest rates in the coming quarters. When you combine all that with the fact that it also trades at a 6.2% discount to its net asset value, KBDC looks like another highly compelling 10.33% dividend deal that should be sustainable for the foreseeable future. Additionally, it offers material capital appreciation potential through both an expanding price-to-NAV ratio as well as the potential for NAV per share growth as its NII is expected to be above its dividend in the coming quarters.

Investor Takeaway

Between MSDL and KBDC, investors get access to defensively positioned, well-managed portfolios of senior-secured investments at material discounts to net asset value. Additionally, these are backed by strong balance sheets with no headwinds from having to refinance debt at higher interest rates for the foreseeable future, as well as double-digit dividend yields that should be very sustainable moving forward.

Additionally, with their focus on floating rate loans, they can provide valuable diversification benefits for a high-yield portfolio invested in interest rate-sensitive securities such as REITs like Realty Income Corporation (O), utilities like NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE), and infrastructure companies like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. Limited Partnership Units (BIP). As a result, we hold positions in both of them in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.
